text: As a method of intracorporeal reconstruction for colonic cancer, isopelastaltic or antiparastaltic stapled anastomosis is usually used. These anastomotic techniques are side-to-side anastomosis. Therefore, sufficient mobilization of the intestine before anastomosis is required in both techniques. Intracorporeal suture to close the entry hole is required in an isoparastaltic anastomosis.
Therefore, a suture-less stapled end-to-end anastomosis will be promising anastomotic technique.

The surgeon uses a laparoscopic stapler to transect the proximal and distal sides of the intestinal tract. Ten millimeter transverse enterotomies are created at the mesenteric edges of the stapling line on both intestines. Each jaw of a forty-five millimeter linear stapler is inserted into the holes along the posterior wall of both intestinal tracts, and the device is closed and fired to complete the anastomosis.

The surgeon closes the entry hole after the initial suturing of both intestinal tracts. The assistant holds the proximal and distal ends of the first suture line. The entry hole is partially closed using a 30mm stapler and then completely closed with a 60mm stapler, completing the intracorporeal suture-less stapled end-to-end anastomosis.

The surgeon examines the dorsal side of the end-to-end anastomosis, noting that the mesentery orientation is consistent on both the proximal and distal sides. The diameter of the anastomosis appears well secured.
The laparoscopic left colectomy is performed with trochars placed in specific positions. The video will demonstrate the anastomosis procedure.

Following the transection of the proximal and distal sides of the intestinal tract with a laparoscopic stapler, endocyanine green is injected intravenously. Laparoscopic near-infrared imaging confirms good blood flow. Ten millimeter transverse enterotomies are created at the mesenteric edges of the stapling lines on both the transverse and descending colon.

The surgeon inserts each jaw of the 45mm linear stapler into the enterotomies along the posterior wall of both intestinal tracts, closes the device, and fires it. Following the stapling, the interior of the anastomosis is inspected to ensure there is no bleeding.
The assistant grasps the proximal and distal ends of the first suture line as the surgeon prepares to close the entry hole, completing the intracorporeal suture-less stapled end-to-end anastomosis.

The surgeon uses an Endo GIA reinforced reload to grasp the ends of the suture line. The entry hole is partially closed with a 30mm stapler and then completely closed with a 60mm stapler to prevent anastomosis stenosis. The stump of the staple is grasped, and the closure is divided into two steps, eliminating the need for suturing. The 60mm linear stapler is used for the second firing to ensure complete closure of the entry hole, with all layers visibly sutured.
The intracorporeal sutureless stapled end-to-end anastomosis is completed, showing the anastomosis site with aligned mesenteric orientation on both the proximal and distal sides.
The postoperative course was uneventful, with the first flatus on the first postoperative day, dietary intake on the second postoperative day, and hospital discharge on the fifth postoperative day.

First, this is an end-to-end anastomosis and the range of mobilization is less than that of previous anastomosis, and it is less likely to get tension. Second, it is physiological because the mesentery is oriented in the same direction. Third, anastomosis time is reduced because no hand zone suture in the abdominal cavity is required. In conclusion, we believe that this anastomosis technique will be an alternative to the conventional side-to-side anastomosis.
